title: What is an Image Filter?
concept: An image filter slides a small window of numbers across a picture, recalculating each pixel from its neighbors — the basic operation behind blurring, sharpening, and edge detection.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is a digital image made of, at its core?

options.text: A grid of numbers, where each one represents a pixel's brightness or color
A single number describing the whole picture
A list of shapes and outlines the computer detects

exercises.explain: Right! Every digital image is a grid of pixel values — and today's filters work by looking at small patches of that grid.

cards.title: The magic "Blur" button
cards.body: Open any photo app, tap "Blur" or "Sharpen," and the image instantly changes. It feels like magic, but underneath, something surprisingly simple is happening: a small grid of numbers is sliding across your photo, pixel by pixel.

cards.title: What is an image filter?
cards.body: An image filter (also called a kernel) is a small grid of numbers — often just 3×3 — that slides across every position in an image. At each stop, it looks at a pixel and its close neighbors, then combines their values into a new result for that spot.

cards.title: A window scanning the whole photo
cards.body: Picture a small window scanning left to right, top to bottom across a photo, like a magnifying glass moving over a grid. At every stop it peeks at the neighborhood of pixels underneath it, does a little math, and writes down one new number before sliding to the next position.

cards.title: Smoothing a grainy photo
cards.body: Take a photo with speckly noise. Slide a 3×3 window across it, and at each stop replace the center pixel with the average of its 9 neighbors. Repeat for every pixel, and the graininess smooths away — that's a filter at work.

cards.title: It's not AI reading the picture
cards.body: It's tempting to think a filter "understands" the photo. It doesn't. A classical filter has no idea what a face or a car is — it only ever looks at raw brightness numbers in a small neighborhood and does fixed, repeatable math.

cards.title: One pixel alone vs. one pixel with neighbors
cards.body: How a filter changes what a pixel "sees":

left.text:
Each pixel keeps its own original value
Ignores everything around it

right.text:
Each pixel is recalculated
using a small window of neighbors

exercises.prompt: What does an image filter (kernel) actually do?

options.text: Slides across the image and recomputes each pixel from its neighborhood
Deletes random pixels to make the file smaller
Identifies objects like faces or cars in the photo

exercises.explain: Exactly — a filter is a small sliding window that recalculates pixel values from their neighbors.

exercises.prompt: True or false: An image filter looks at every pixel in the entire image at once to decide a new value for one pixel.
exercises.explain: False. A filter only looks at a small local neighborhood around each pixel, not the whole image at once.

exercises.sentence: A filter is a small grid of numbers, often called a ___, that slides across the image.
exercises.explain: Kernel is correct — it's the standard name for the small sliding grid a filter uses.

exercises.prompt: Which of these is an example of a classical image filter at work?

options.text: Sliding a 3×3 window across a photo and averaging each neighborhood to smooth it
A model recognizing the make and model of a car in the photo
Compressing the photo into a smaller JPEG file

exercises.explain: The first option describes the core filter operation: a small window sliding across pixels doing local math.

exercises.prompt: A photo editing app has a "Blur" slider. Under the hood, what is most likely happening as you increase it?

options.text: A larger sliding window is averaging more neighboring pixels together for each pixel
The app is asking a neural network to redraw the photo from scratch
The app is deleting pixels near the edges of the photo
The app is switching the image to a different file format

exercises.explain: Exactly! A bigger blur usually means a bigger averaging window. Next: let's zoom into exactly how that averaging works.
